chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: ISEN 340
Course Title: Operations Research II
Section: 502
Time: TR 3:55 PM-05:10 PM
Location: ETB 1013
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Probabilistic methods for industrial and service systems; stochastic processes used in industrial engineering, including Poisson processes and discrete and continuous-time Markov chains; applications to production operations, inventory control, revenue management, quality control, reliability, digital simulation and finance.

This is a required course for all ISEN majors.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in MATH 304 or MATH 323; grade of C or better in ISEN 310, STAT 212, or ECEN 303; junior or senior classification.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Ability to choose and develop probability model based on verbal description of decision making scenario
• Ability to collect and analyze data to specify probability model parameters
• Ability to encode probability model into software and solve for needed values
• Ability to interpret model results and use judgement to make decisions

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Modeling Random Processes for Engineers and Managers, by James J. Solberg. Wiley, 2009.

Practical Reliability Engineering, 5th Edition, by Patrick O'Connor and Andre Kleyner, Wiley, 2012.

The course requires matrix operations such as multiplication, inverse, and additions. In this course, we will be using the Python programming language in Jupyter Notebooks. Some notebooks will be provided with code. You will be expected to understand and use the code to solve problems on homework, quizzes, and tests. And you may be asked to do a limited amount of Python programming. So make sure you have Jupyter installed on your computer and bring your computer to class.

chunks.title: Course Policies
chunks.content:
• There will be 6 quizzes and a final exam, administered in Canvas.
• Questions will be a mixture of True/False, multiple choice, and multiple selection.
• Quizzes / the final may be taken only in class or in the testing center and will be closed book, closed notes.
• Quizzes will be on specified topics, the final will be comprehensive.
• For each quiz, you should prepare one 8.5x11 handwritten (both sides) cheat sheet to use during the quiz. You must prepare your own cheat sheet, in your own handwriting.
• For each quiz, you may use all cheat sheets prepared for previous quizzes as well as the cheat sheet prepared for that quiz.
• On the final, you may use all the cheat sheets you prepared for quizzes.
• For some quiz/test questions, you will need to use a specified jupyter notebook. On the day of a quiz or final exam, make sure your computer is running and jupyter is loaded before you come to class.
• Quizzes will be scheduled on selected Thursdays at 4:30pm. You will have until the end of class to finish (5:10PM).
• Plenty of homework will be assigned to prepare you for the quizzes / final, but it will not be submitted. Solutions will be provided a day or so before the topical quiz so that you may check your work. It is expected that you will attempt all homework and seek help if you have trouble.
• At the end of the semester, if your score on the 6 quizzes is 100%, you will be excused from the final exam. Otherwise, your lowest quiz score will be dropped in computing your final grade.
• Grading will be based on the following:
| | |
|:---|:---|
| 1. Quizzes | 80% |
| 2. Final Exam | 20% |
• Letter grades will be assigned according to this scale:
| | |
|:---|:---|
| 100% Quiz Scores | ~ A |
| 90-100% | ~ A |
| 80-89% | ~ B |
| 70-79% | ~ C |
| 60-69% | ~ D |
| < 60% | ~ F |

chunks.title: Class Attendence
chunks.content:
You are expected to attend classes.
• Attendence will be monitored.
• If you are not attending class on a regular basis, you will be asked to meet with the professor to discuss the situation.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• You can make up a missed quiz only with a valid excuse. (Student Rule 7)
• If you miss a quiz without a valid excuse, you will not be allowed a make up, but please remember that the lowest quiz score will be dropped.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Week 1 (1/13, 1/15) Introduction and Probability Review (Solberg Ch 1)
Week 2 (1/20, 1/22) Probability Review
Week 3 (1/27, 1/29) Formulating Markov Chain Models (Solberg Ch 2)
Quiz 1 Thursday January 29, Conditional Probability
Week 4-5 (2/3, 2/5, 2/10, 2/12) Markov Chain Calculations (Solberg Ch 3)
Quiz 2 Thursday February 12, Markov Chain Models
Week 6-7 (2/17, 2/19, 2/24, 2/26) Markov Chain Calculations (Solberg Ch 3)
Week 8 (3/3, 3/5) Continuous Time Markov Chains (Solberg Ch 5)
Quiz 3 Thursday March 5, Markov Chain Computations
Spring Break (3/10, 3/12)
Week 9 (3/17, 3/19) Continuous Time Markov Chains (Solberg Ch 5)
Week 10 (3/24, 3/26) Queueing Models (Solberg, Ch 6)
Quiz 4 Thursday March 26, Continuous Time Markov Chains
Week 11-12 (3/31, 4/2, 4/7, 4/9) Queueing Models / Networks of Queues (Solberg, Ch 6, 7)
Quiz 5 Thursday April 9, Queueing Models
Week 13 (4/14, 4/16) Reliability Engineering – Distribution Fitting & Life Estimation
Week 14 (4/21, 4/23) Reliability Engineering – System Reliability Design
Quiz 6 Thursday April 23, Networks of Queues & Reliability Engineering
Week 15 (4/28) Review
Last Day of Class Monday April 28
Final Exam Monday May 4, 502 1:00 – 3:00PM
